old_claim.note: Psychology provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Human-factors and cognitive-performance research informed GUI design choices such as icon size, grouping, and visual-search efficiency; psychology is enabling design evidence rather than a mandatory component.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract reports a 490-subject visual-search experiment on graphical object size/grouping and task efficiency, explicitly applying the results to graphical user-computer interface design and implementation.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source demonstrates that user performance with GUI objects can be experimentally measured and used during graphical-interface design and implementation.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That empirical human-performance loop is the local reason psychology remains an enabling design science for GUIs, without implying that the whole psychology discipline is a hard technical component.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: graphical_user_interface_gui keeps psychology as an enabling human-factors edge with a narrowed user-performance rationale.
would_reject_if: The GUI node were narrowed to purely rendering-library internals with no user-interaction design scope.
A stronger source showed GUI design practice did not use human-performance, perception, or usability research.
A narrower human_factors_engineering node were added and should replace the broad psychology prerequisite.
short_reason: GUI design uses empirical human-performance evidence.
